meta.note: Full sample stories with craft-annotation layer. Each = {id, title, question, paragraphs:[{text, note}]}. 'note' is the craft x-ray tip for that paragraph (shown when annotation toggle is ON). Leave note empty for no annotation. Fill with your own stories.

samples.title: The Postman
samples.question: When he saw the postman coming up to his door, Matt ran to open it.

paragraphs.text: When he saw the postman coming up to his door, Matt ran to open it. For weeks he had waited for one thing: the new mobile phone he had saved up for all summer. His hands shook as he tore open the parcel.
paragraphs.note: SCENE (Step 1 — how it started): begins with the exact given line, then explains why Matt is so eager. The required mobile phone appears at once, with 'shook with excitement' setting the mood.

paragraphs.text: As soon as the phone was charged, Matt began exploring it. While he was setting it up, a message suddenly appeared from an unknown number. 'Hi! I think you have my old number,' it read. Curious, he replied, and before long the two were chatting like old friends.
paragraphs.note: DEVELOP (Step 2 — what happened): time connectives move the action on — 'As soon as', 'While he was setting it up', 'before long'. The mysterious message drives the story forward.

paragraphs.text: The next thing Matt knew, they had agreed to meet at the park. No sooner had he arrived than a nervous boy his own age waved at him. To his surprise, it was Sam, who had just moved to the area and knew nobody.
paragraphs.note: TENSION → the NEW FRIEND (Step 3 — details): the interrupters 'The next thing' and 'No sooner had… than…' sharpen the turning point, delivering the required new friend — Sam.

paragraphs.text: Luckily, what had begun as a wrong number had become something wonderful. Fortunately, Matt and Sam soon became best friends. To Matt's relief, the summer he had feared would be lonely became his best ever. Looking back, the phone had brought him more than he expected.
paragraphs.note: RESOLUTION (Step 4 — how it ended): the relief-closers 'Luckily', 'Fortunately', 'To Matt's relief' resolve the story, and 'Looking back' gives the reflective final touch.

samples.title: The Diving Lesson
samples.question: It was Tom's first deep-sea diving lesson and he was quite nervous.

paragraphs.text: It was Tom's first deep-sea diving lesson and he was quite nervous. The water was cold and impossibly blue, and his heart pounded as he sank slowly beneath the waves. All around him, silver fish darted between the swaying weeds.
paragraphs.note: SCENE (Step 1 — how it started): begins with the exact given line, then sets the underwater scene with atmosphere adjectives — 'cold', 'impossibly blue' — and 'heart pounded' carries his nervousness.

paragraphs.text: As he was admiring the reef, a huge dark shape suddenly appeared out of the gloom. It was a shark, gliding straight towards him. While Tom froze in terror, his instructor calmly raised a hand, signalling him to stay perfectly still.
paragraphs.note: DEVELOP (Step 2 — what happened): time connectives 'As he was admiring' and 'While Tom froze' move the action on; the required shark arrives, and vivid verbs (appeared, gliding, froze) build the danger.

paragraphs.text: No sooner had the shark drawn near than it turned lazily and swam away. The next thing Tom knew, his instructor was pointing at an underwater camera clipped to his belt. Heart still racing, Tom lifted it and took a photograph of the great creature disappearing into the blue.
paragraphs.note: TENSION → the PHOTOGRAPH (Step 3 — details): the interrupters 'No sooner had… than…' and 'The next thing' sharpen the turning point, delivering the required photograph.

paragraphs.text: Luckily, the shark had been completely harmless. Fortunately, Tom's photograph turned out to be spectacular. To his relief, the lesson he had dreaded became the greatest adventure of his life. Looking back, he could hardly believe he had once been afraid of the sea.
paragraphs.note: RESOLUTION (Step 4 — how it ended): the relief-closers 'Luckily', 'Fortunately', 'To his relief' resolve the story, and 'Looking back' gives the reflective final touch.

samples.title: The Mountain Trek
samples.question: Jack and Pedro were trekking through the mountains on the first day of their holidays.

paragraphs.text: Jack and Pedro were trekking through the mountains on the first day of their holidays. The morning was bright and warm, and the path wound upwards through sweet-smelling pines. Laughing and joking, they had never felt so free.
paragraphs.note: SCENE (Step 1 — how it started): begins with the exact given line, then sets the mountain scene with atmosphere adjectives — 'bright and warm', 'sweet-smelling pines' — and a carefree mood.

paragraphs.text: After a while, the sky began to change. While they were climbing a steep ridge, black clouds suddenly rolled in from the west. Before long, rain hammered down and a cold wind howled around them. The boys realised, with a shock, that they were completely exposed.
paragraphs.note: DEVELOP (Step 2 — what happened): time connectives 'After a while', 'While they were climbing', 'Before long' move the action on; the required black clouds arrive, and vivid verbs (rolled, hammered, howled) build the danger.

paragraphs.text: No sooner had the storm broken than Pedro spotted a dark opening in the rocks. The next thing they knew, they were scrambling into a narrow cave, soaked but safe. Huddled together in the dark, they waited as thunder crashed outside.
paragraphs.note: TENSION → the CAVE (Step 3 — details): the interrupters 'No sooner had… than…' and 'The next thing' sharpen the turning point, delivering the required cave as shelter.

paragraphs.text: Luckily, the storm passed as quickly as it had come. Fortunately, the boys emerged unharmed into brilliant sunshine. To their relief, the frightening morning had become an adventure they would never forget. Looking back, they agreed it was the best start to a holiday they could have wished for.
paragraphs.note: RESOLUTION (Step 4 — how it ended): the relief-closers 'Luckily', 'Fortunately', 'To their relief' resolve the story, and 'Looking back' gives the reflective final touch.

samples.title: The Flight
samples.question: Feeling slightly nervous, Emma walked up the steps and onto the plane.

paragraphs.text: Feeling slightly nervous, Emma walked up the steps and onto the plane. It was a cold, grey morning, and heavy clouds hung low over the airport. She had never flown alone before, and as she found her seat, her heart was beating fast.
paragraphs.note: SCENE (Step 1 — how it started): begins with the exact given line, then sets time and mood with atmosphere adjectives — 'cold, grey morning', 'heavy clouds'. The bad weather is woven in from the very start.

paragraphs.text: After a while, the engines roared and the plane climbed into the storm. While they were flying through the thick cloud, the aircraft suddenly began to shake. Rain hammered against the windows and, all around her, passengers gripped their seats in silence.
paragraphs.note: DEVELOP (Step 2 — what happened): time connectives move the action on — 'After a while', 'While they were flying'. Vivid verbs (roared, shake, hammered) turn the bad weather into real danger.

paragraphs.text: No sooner had the captain spoken than the shaking stopped. The next thing Emma knew, a flight attendant was smiling down at her. 'Are you Emma? Someone on this flight has been looking for you.' Three rows ahead, waving, sat her grandmother — whom she had not seen for five years.
paragraphs.note: TENSION → SURPRISE (Step 3 — details): the interrupter 'No sooner had… than…' and 'The next thing' sharpen the turning point, then deliver the required surprise — the unexpected grandmother.

paragraphs.text: Luckily, the rest of the flight was smooth. Fortunately, Emma spent the whole journey laughing with her grandmother, the storm forgotten. To her relief, the frightening start had led to the happiest surprise of her life. Looking back, she knew she would never feel nervous about flying again.
paragraphs.note: RESOLUTION (Step 4 — how it ended): the relief-closers 'Luckily', 'Fortunately', 'To her relief' resolve the tension, and 'Looking back' adds the reflective final touch.
